summary: High-quality golden-based replay test that genuinely proves the version-aware bgwriter layout switch (PG14/17/18) without a live PostgreSQL. The golden-equality assertion is load-bearing: it pins exact diffed deltas (curr-prev), absolute pass-through columns, the new PG18 slru_written column, and the per-version column count/order. Litmus test passes for all three subcases — breaking calculateDelta (return curr verbatim) or inverting the version switch would cause golden mismatch. All acceptance points from the task are covered. Findings are minor only.

findings.issue: The timestamp-header regex assertion (`assert.Regexp(... \d{4}/\d{2}/\d{2} ...)`) is fully subsumed by the golden-equality assertion at line 230: the golden's line 2 already contains the literal '2026/05/19 10:00:01, rate: 1s' header, so byte-for-byte equality already proves the header is present and correct. The extra regex catches no failure the golden comparison would miss.
findings.recommendation: Optional: drop the regex assertion, or — to mirror the procpidstat harness model (report_test.go:710-715) — replace it with an assertion that pins the data row independently of the golden, e.g. assert.Contains(t, out, "Bgwriter") plus a per-version delta sentinel. This is purely defensive redundancy and not required for correctness.

findings.issue: Golden files embed raw ANSI color escape sequences ([37;1m) and are regenerated via the -update flag, which is the snapshot-abuse risk class (large machine-generated blobs that reviewers may not eyeball). Here the blobs are tiny (4 lines each) and the diffed values were independently verified to encode correct curr-prev deltas (e.g. buf_ckpt 1500-1000=500, slru_written 90-50=40), so the risk is low and the project-wide convention (report_wal.golden et al.) is followed consistently.
findings.recommendation: No change needed; documenting the residual snapshot-review risk. Keep golden files small and ensure any future -update regeneration is reviewed by re-deriving at least one delta per version by hand, as was done here.
